subtopics.title: Introduction to Object-Oriented Programming – Why OOP?

blocks.title: What is Object-Oriented Programming?
blocks.content: OOP is a programming paradigm that organizes software design around objects rather than functions and logic. An object is a self-contained entity that contains both data (attributes) and behaviour (methods).

rows.right: Data and functions are encapsulated together.

blocks.title: Key OOP Concepts
steps: Step 2: Object – Instance of a class.
Step 4: Inheritance – Reusing code from parent classes.
Step 5: Polymorphism – Many forms of a single interface.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: OOP models real-world entities as objects.
Objects have properties (attributes) and behaviours (methods).
The four pillars of OOP: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction.
OOP promotes code reuse, modularity, and maintainability.

subtopics.title: Classes and Objects – The Building Blocks

blocks.title: What is a Class?
blocks.content: A class is a blueprint or template that defines the properties and behaviours that objects of that class will have. It does not occupy memory until an object is created.

blocks.title: What is an Object?
blocks.content: An object is an instance of a class. It occupies memory and has actual values for its properties. Multiple objects can be created from a single class.

blocks.title: Example: Class and Object
given: Define a class 'Car' with attributes brand, model, and year.
blocks.find: Create an object of the class.
blocks.answer: myCar is an object of the Car class.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing Class and Object.
blocks.wrong: Class is an instance of an object.
blocks.correct: An object is an instance of a class.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Class: Blueprint, no memory allocation.
Object: Instance, memory allocated.
Objects have state (attributes) and behaviour (methods).
Multiple objects can be created from one class.

blocks.title: What is Encapsulation?
blocks.content: Encapsulation is the mechanism of wrapping data (attributes) and methods (behaviour) together as a single unit and restricting direct access to data. It is achieved using access modifiers (private, public, protected).

blocks.title: Benefits of Encapsulation
blocks.content:
1. **Data Hiding** – Internal details are hidden from outside.
2. **Security** – Prevents unauthorized access and modification.
3. **Maintainability** – Internal implementation can change without affecting outside code.
4. **Modularity** – Each class is a self-contained unit.

blocks.title: Example: Encapsulation in Java
blocks.answer: balance is private and accessed only through methods.

blocks.title: Trap: Making all attributes public.
blocks.correct: private int age; with public getter/setter.
blocks.tip: Always use private attributes and provide access through methods.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Encapsulation = Wrapping data + methods into a single unit.
Access modifiers control visibility.
Private = hidden, Public = accessible, Protected = inheritance.
Data hiding protects integrity.

subtopics.title: Inheritance – Reusing Code from Parent Classes

blocks.title: What is Inheritance?
blocks.content: Inheritance is a mechanism where a new class (child/subclass) derives properties and behaviours from an existing class (parent/superclass). It promotes code reuse and establishes a hierarchical relationship.

blocks.title: Types of Inheritance
rows: One child class inherits from one parent.
Class inherits from a class that inherits from another.
Multiple child classes inherit from one parent.
Not supported in Java/C#, supported in C++

blocks.title: Example: Inheritance in Java
given: Parent class 'Vehicle' with a method start().
blocks.find: Create a child class 'Car' that inherits from 'Vehicle'.
blocks.answer: Car inherits start() from Vehicle and adds honk().

blocks.title: Trap: Multiple inheritance is supported in all languages.
blocks.wrong: Multiple inheritance is supported in Java.
blocks.correct: Java does not support multiple inheritance of classes (uses interfaces instead).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Inheritance = Code reuse + Hierarchical classification.
Subclass inherits all non-private members.
Use 'extends' in Java/C#, ':' in C++.
Multiple inheritance is not supported in Java/C#.

subtopics.title: Polymorphism – Many Forms of a Single Interface

blocks.title: What is Polymorphism?
blocks.content: Polymorphism means 'many forms'. It allows objects of different classes to be treated as objects of a common superclass, and each can respond to the same method call in its own way.

blocks.title: Types of Polymorphism
rows: Method overloading – multiple methods with same name but different parameters.
void add(int a, int b) and void add(int a, int b, int c)
Method overriding – subclass provides its own implementation of a superclass method.

blocks.title: Example: Method Overloading (Compile-Time)
steps: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; }
double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
blocks.answer: Three overloaded methods with different signatures.

blocks.title: Example: Method Overriding (Run-Time)
blocks.answer: Dog overrides sound() with its own implementation.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing Overloading and Overriding.
blocks.wrong: Overloading and overriding are the same.
blocks.correct: Overloading = same method name, different parameters (compile-time). Overriding = same method signature, different implementation (run-time).
blocks.tip: Overloading happens in the same class; Overriding happens in subclasses.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Polymorphism = Many forms.
Overloading (compile-time): Same name, different parameters.
Overriding (run-time): Subclass replaces parent method.
Polymorphism enables flexible and extensible code.

blocks.title: What is Abstraction?
blocks.content: Abstraction is the process of hiding implementation details and exposing only the essential features of an object. It reduces complexity by focusing on what the object does rather than how it does it.

blocks.title: Abstraction in Java/C#
blocks.content:
1. **Abstract Classes** – Cannot be instantiated, may have abstract methods (no body).
2. **Interfaces** – 100% abstract (before Java 8), define a contract for classes to implement.

blocks.title: Example: Abstract Class
given: A class 'Shape' with an abstract method area().
blocks.answer: Circle and Rectangle provide concrete implementations.

blocks.title: Example: Interface
given: An interface 'Drawable' with a method draw().

blocks.title: Trap: Abstract classes can be instantiated.
blocks.wrong: Abstract classes can be instantiated using new.
blocks.correct: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Abstraction = Hiding implementation, exposing interface.
Abstract classes: cannot be instantiated, may have abstract methods.
Interfaces: define contracts for classes to implement.
Abstraction reduces complexity and increases flexibility.

blocks.title: What is a Constructor?
blocks.content: A constructor is a special method that is automatically called when an object is created. It initializes the object's attributes and performs any setup tasks.

blocks.title: Types of Constructors
rows: No parameters, provided automatically if no constructor is defined.
public Car(String b, String m, int y) { brand = b; model = m; year = y; }
Creates an object by copying another object.
Same class with default and parameterized constructors.

blocks.title: Example: Constructors
given: A class 'Student' with name and age.
steps: Student(String n, int a) { name = n; age = a; }
blocks.answer: Both constructors are defined and used.

blocks.title: Trap: Constructor name must match class name.
blocks.wrong: public void Student() { ... } (void makes it a method, not a constructor).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Constructor = Special method for object initialization.
Same name as the class, no return type.
Default constructor (no parameters) is provided automatically if none is defined.
Constructor overloading allows multiple ways to create objects.

subtopics.title: The 'this' and 'super' Keywords – Referring to Members

blocks.title: The 'this' Keyword
blocks.content: 'this' refers to the current object. It is used to distinguish instance variables from parameters, and to call other constructors of the same class.

blocks.title: The 'super' Keyword
blocks.content: 'super' refers to the parent class. It is used to call parent class methods, constructors, and access parent class members.

blocks.title: Example: 'this' Keyword
given: A class with a parameter same name as an attribute.
steps: this.name = name; // this.name = attribute, name = parameter

blocks.title: Example: 'super' Keyword
given: Child class overrides a parent method.

blocks.title: Trap: Using 'this' in a static context.
blocks.wrong: static void method() { this.print(); } (this cannot be used in static).
blocks.tip: 'this' is not available in static methods.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: 'this' = current object reference.
'super' = parent class reference.
Use 'this' to distinguish attributes from parameters.
Use 'super' to call parent methods and constructors.

subtopics.title: SOLID Principles – Design for Maintainable Code

blocks.title: What are SOLID Principles?
blocks.content: SOLID is an acronym for five design principles that help developers create more maintainable, understandable, and flexible software. They were introduced by Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob).

blocks.title: SOLID Principles
rows: A class should have only one reason to change.
A class should be open for extension but closed for modification.
Add new features without changing existing code.
Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types.
Child class should be able to replace parent.
Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they don't use.
High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions.
Depend on interfaces, not concrete classes.

blocks.title: Example: Single Responsibility
given: A class 'Report' that calculates and prints data.
steps: Now each class has only one reason to change.

blocks.title: Example: Open/Closed Principle
given: A class 'AreaCalculator' that calculates area of shapes.
steps: abstract class Shape { abstract double area(); }
blocks.answer: New shapes can be added without changing AreaCalculator.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing SOLID principles.
blocks.correct: Liskov Substitution is about inheritance and subtype substitutability.
blocks.tip: Study each principle in detail to avoid confusion.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: S: Single Responsibility – One job per class.
O: Open/Closed – Extend without modifying.
L: Liskov Substitution – Subtypes must work where base types work.
I: Interface Segregation – Separate large interfaces.
D: Dependency Inversion – Depend on abstractions.

blocks.title: Inheritance (Is-a Relationship)
blocks.content: Inheritance represents an 'is-a' relationship. Example: A Dog 'is-a' Animal. Subclasses inherit from a superclass. It is a strong relationship but can lead to tight coupling.

blocks.title: Composition (Has-a Relationship)
blocks.content: Composition represents a 'has-a' relationship. Example: A Car 'has-a' Engine. An object contains references to other objects. It is more flexible and loosely coupled than inheritance.

blocks.title: Trap: Overusing inheritance.
blocks.wrong: Using inheritance when composition would be better.
blocks.tip: Use inheritance only when the relationship is truly 'is-a'.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Inheritance = Is-a (Dog is an Animal).
Composition = Has-a (Car has an Engine).
Composition is more flexible and loosely coupled.
Favor composition over inheritance.

blocks.title: What is an Interface?
blocks.content: An interface defines a contract that implementing classes must fulfill. It declares methods but does not provide implementations. Interfaces support multiple inheritance of behaviour.

blocks.title: Interface Characteristics
rows: All methods are abstract (in Java 8, default/static methods allowed).
All fields are public, static, final (constants).
A class can implement multiple interfaces.
Defines a contract for classes to implement.

blocks.title: Example: Multiple Interfaces
given: A class 'Bird' that can fly and swim.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Interface = Contract for classes.
All methods are abstract (before Java 8).
A class can implement multiple interfaces.
Interfaces support multiple inheritance of behaviour.

subtopics.title: Advanced OOP Concepts – Aggregation, Association, and Delegation

blocks.title: Association
blocks.content: Association is a relationship between two classes where one class uses the other. It is a general relationship (not ownership). Example: A Teacher teaches a Student.

blocks.title: Aggregation
blocks.content: Aggregation is a weak 'has-a' relationship where the child can exist independently of the parent. Example: A Department has Employees (employees can exist without the department).

blocks.title: Composition (Strong Aggregation)
blocks.content: Composition is a strong 'has-a' relationship where the child cannot exist without the parent. Example: A House has Rooms (rooms cannot exist without the house).

blocks.title: Delegation
blocks.content: Delegation is a technique where an object delegates certain responsibilities to another object. It is an alternative to inheritance for code reuse.

blocks.title: Example: Delegation
given: A class 'Printer' needs to print documents.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Association = General connection between classes.
Aggregation = Weak ownership (child can exist alone).
Composition = Strong ownership (child cannot exist alone).
Delegation = Forwarding responsibilities to another object.

subtopics.title: Shortcuts and Memory Tricks for OOP Concepts

blocks.title: Shortcut 1 – The Four Pillars
blocks.content: EIPA: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction.

blocks.title: Shortcut 2 – Class vs Object
blocks.content: Class = Blueprint (no memory). Object = Instance (has memory).

blocks.title: Shortcut 3 – Overloading vs Overriding
blocks.content: Overloading = Same name, different parameters (compile-time). Overriding = Same signature, different implementation (run-time).

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Encapsulation, Polymorphism, Inheritance, Abstraction – the four pillars of OOP.

blocks.title: Shortcut Summary
points: E: Encapsulation = Data hiding.
P: Polymorphism = Many forms (overloading + overriding).
I: Inheritance = Code reuse.
A: Abstraction = Hiding implementation.
Class = Blueprint, Object = Instance.
this = current object, super = parent object.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Focus on the four pillars (Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction). Understand the difference between overloading and overriding. Know SOLID principles and constructors.

blocks.question: Which OOP principle hides internal details and protects data?
blocks.explanation: Encapsulation hides internal details and protects data from unauthorized access.

blocks.question: Which OOP principle allows a class to have multiple methods with the same name but different parameters?
blocks.explanation: Method Overloading allows methods with the same name but different parameters (compile-time polymorphism).

blocks.question: What is the process of creating a new class from an existing class called?
blocks.explanation: Inheritance is the process of creating a new class (child) from an existing class (parent).

blocks.question: Which keyword is used to refer to the parent class in Java?
blocks.explanation: 'super' is used to refer to the parent class in Java.

blocks.question: Which OOP principle allows objects of different classes to respond to the same method call in different ways?
blocks.explanation: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.

blocks.question: Which principle states that a class should have only one reason to change?
blocks.explanation: Single Responsibility Principle states that a class should have only one reason to change.

blocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature of an interface in Java?
blocks.explanation: Interfaces cannot be instantiated. They are implemented by classes.

blocks.question: What is the difference between overloading and overriding?
options: Overloading is run-time, overriding is compile-time.
Overloading is compile-time, overriding is run-time.
blocks.explanation: Overloading is compile-time polymorphism; overriding is run-time polymorphism.

blocks.question: Which relationship describes 'has-a'?
blocks.explanation: Composition represents a 'has-a' relationship (e.g., Car has an Engine).

blocks.question: Which SOLID principle states that high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules?
blocks.explanation: Dependency Inversion Principle: Depend on abstractions, not concretions.

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Review the four pillars of OOP. Understand the difference between overloading and overriding. Study SOLID principles and the difference between inheritance and composition.

blocks.title: Final Revision – OOP Concepts
points: Encapsulation: Data hiding, private attributes.
Inheritance: Code reuse, is-a relationship.
Polymorphism: Overloading (compile-time), Overriding (run-time).
Abstraction: Abstract classes and interfaces.
this: Current object, super: Parent object.
Constructors: Initialization of objects.
SOLID: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion.
Composition: Has-a, more flexible than inheritance.
Interfaces: Contracts for classes, support multiple inheritance.
